result:
# The Texture of Waiting

I want to write about waiting. Not the dramatic kind — not waiting for biopsy results or a verdict or a phone call that will change everything. I mean the ordinary, unremarkable waiting that fills up most of a life. The waiting at bus stops. The waiting for water to boil. The twenty seconds standing in front of an open refrigerator, not hungry exactly, but not not-hungry either, looking for something you can't name.

I think waiting gets a bad reputation because we've decided it's the opposite of living. We talk about killing time, wasting time, time that needs to be gotten through. We treat the gaps between events as unfortunate administrative overhead — the loading screens of a life. But I wonder if we've got it exactly backwards. I wonder if the waiting is where most of the actual texture of existence lives.

Consider the bus stop. You're standing there with four or five strangers. Nobody is talking. Everybody is looking at a phone or looking down the street or studying the middle distance with an expression of profound neutrality. This scene gets described, when it gets described at all, as alienation. Modern loneliness. The death of community. And maybe there's something to that. But there's also something else happening that I find genuinely interesting.

Each person at that bus stop is somewhere. Not physically — I mean mentally, emotionally, wherever the mind goes when the body is just standing still. One person is having an argument in their head that they should have had last Tuesday. One person is running through a grocery list and realizing they've forgotten what it was they needed most. One person is thinking about their father, who is getting older, who seemed smaller last visit somehow, who told a joke that didn't quite land and laughed anyway. One person is not thinking about anything at all in the way that is actually a very specific kind of thinking, an active blanking, a deliberate low-pressure system where thoughts form and immediately dissipate.

All of this is invisible. Every person at the bus stop is hosting an entire interior weather system and nobody can see any of it. And this isn't remarkable to us, because it's true everywhere, all the time — but somehow the flatness of the waiting scenario makes it more visible. When people are doing things, there's a story about what they're doing. When they're waiting, there's no cover story. You see more clearly that there's an inside.

---

I've been thinking lately about how we experience time differently depending on our relationship to memory and anticipation. Young children experience waiting as almost unbearably long. A car ride of two hours is an eternity. The week before a birthday is geological. This is partly because their sense of duration is calibrated against a shorter total lifespan — a week when you're seven years old is a proportionally enormous slice of your conscious existence. But it's also because they haven't yet developed the mental machinery for making time pass. They don't have the infrastructure of distraction or the habit of letting the mind drift into neutral. They are fully present for every minute of it, which sounds like a spiritual achievement but when you're waiting for Christmas it is actually a mild form of suffering.

Adults learn to make time disappear. We get very good at it. We develop elaborate practices — the podcast on the commute, the book in the waiting room, the habit of rehearsing conversations or planning meals or doing the soft mental bookkeeping of a life. We learn to be absent while physically present. And mostly this is fine, this is how you function, but sometimes I wonder what we're not noticing while we're busy not noticing things.

There's a particular quality of attention that only becomes available during genuine, unmediated waiting. I'm talking about waiting where you've forgotten your phone, or the battery died, or you've made some unusual resolution to just sit there. The first few minutes are uncomfortable. The mind casts around for something to do and finds nothing and this produces a vague anxiety, like the feeling of reaching into a pocket and finding it empty. But if you stay with it — and this is not easy, and I'm not recommending it as some kind of productivity hack or mindfulness exercise, just describing what happens — something shifts.

You start actually seeing things. Not noticing them in the ambient way you're always vaguely aware of your surroundings, but really looking. The water stain on the ceiling that is shaped like nothing in particular but which you now spend three minutes studying anyway. The way the woman across from you is holding her coffee cup with both hands even though it must have long gone cold. The pattern of light through a window that's doing something genuinely beautiful that nobody else seems to be registering.

Or you start thinking in a different register. Not the efficient, goal-directed thinking of a busy person who has allocated some cognitive resources to a problem. Something more associative. Slower. A thought leads to a memory leads to a question leads to something that isn't quite a thought yet, more like a mood or an intimation. This is the kind of thinking that tends to produce things — not in the sense of output, deliverables, solutions, but in the sense of a faint enrichment, a slight deepening of your relationship with your own experience.

---

I want to talk about the specific waiting of illness. I don't mean the waiting for dramatic results I set aside at the beginning — I mean the long, formless waiting of chronic conditions, the waiting of recovery, the waiting of a body that has become unreliable and slow. I know this kind of waiting a little, and I've watched others know it much more intimately.

When you're sick, time does strange things. The hours are very long. The days are somehow simultaneously long and blurred. There's a uniformity to sick days that makes them hard to tell apart in memory, which means that in retrospect a week of illness can seem like almost nothing — a brief dark smear in the timeline — even though living through it felt interminable.

People who are sick often describe a frustration at being taken out of the current of normal life. Things are happening without them. Work, events, the ordinary motion of the world. They are in a waiting room while the action is elsewhere. And I've noticed that this framing — being outside the action — is one of the things that makes illness feel like loss even on days when you're not in pain, even when you're just lying there, tired, watching the light change on the wall.

But some people, some of the time, find something else in it. The enforced stillness strips away a lot of the noise. You stop performing health and capability and busyness. You're just a person in a body, and the body is making its needs very clear, and there's a kind of honesty to that. I've heard people describe a strange gratitude for what they noticed during long illnesses — not for the illness itself, but for the waiting it required. The long thinking. The rediscovery of certain small things. The conversations that happened because there was nothing to do but talk.

I don't want to romanticize this. Illness is often terrible and lonely and frightening and there's nothing noble about suffering. But I think it's true that waiting — including the waiting of a sick body — has a capacity to reveal things that the normal velocity of life covers over.

---

There's a writer I love who described boredom as a necessary precondition for creativity. The idea being that the mind, given nothing to process, turns inward and starts making things. Children left alone with no screens and no structured activity will eventually, after an initial period of restless dissatisfaction, begin to play. They build something from nothing. They invent games, stories, small private mythologies. The nothing becomes something.

Adults have mostly lost the ability to reach that threshold. We fill the nothing before it can become something. We are brilliant preventers of boredom and I think this has costs we don't fully understand.

What I notice in myself — and I say this without much pride — is that some of my best thinking, the thinking I most want to have, happens in the shower, or on long walks without earbuds, or in that specific hypnagogic state just before sleep. These are all waiting states, in a way. The shower is waiting to be clean. The walk is waiting to arrive. The pre-sleep is waiting for sleep. And in these states the mind does something it doesn't do when I'm being productive, which is that it makes unexpected connections. It finds the thread between two things I didn't know were related. It asks a question I didn't know I had.

I can't force this. I can't sit down with a blank document and replicate what happens in the shower. I can only create the conditions and then wait.

---

I think the deepest waiting, the most human waiting, is the kind we do at the edges of other people's lives. Waiting with someone who is sick. Waiting for someone to come home. Waiting in the peculiar suspended state that follows a major loss, when the event has happened but the new shape of the world hasn't quite solidified yet.

This kind of waiting is love, partly. Love is substantially a waiting — a sustained attention to another person, a readiness, a held space. When you love someone you hold them in mind even when they're absent. You keep a kind of interior awareness trained in their direction. This is effortful in a way that doesn't feel like effort, the way holding a favorite book isn't heavy.

When you wait with someone rather than for them — when you sit with a person who is suffering, or frightened, or in the slow endurance of something hard — you're doing something that doesn't have an efficient description. You're providing presence, which sounds modest but is one of the more difficult and important things one person can do for another. There is no technique for it. You just wait alongside. You let the time be what it is. You resist the urge to fix or fill or accelerate.

The hardest waiting of all might be the waiting that accompanies grief. After a death, particularly a sudden one, there can be a long period in which the bereaved person is waiting without quite knowing what they're waiting for. Not for the person to come back — they know better than that — but for the world to feel real again. For the loss to settle into a shape that the rest of life can organize itself around. This waiting has no reliable timeline. It can't be rushed. The people around the grieving person often become very uncomfortable with this waiting and try to do something about it, which is understandable, because the helplessness of waiting with someone is very uncomfortable. But usually the most helpful thing is the presence itself. The willingness to wait alongside.

---

I want to end with something smaller, because I've been getting fairly serious and I think the most true things about waiting are ordinary things.

There's a specific pleasure in certain kinds of waiting that I don't think gets acknowledged enough. Waiting for food when you're genuinely hungry. Waiting for a film to start when you're already in the seat with your coat off and you've given over the next two hours to someone else's story. Waiting for a friend you haven't seen in a long time, watching the arrivals door at an airport, every opening of it a small suspension and then a release.

These waitings are pleasurable because of their structure. They have a known end point, a promised reward, and the waiting itself becomes a kind of savoring. Anticipation is a form of experience, not just a prelude to it. The smell of something cooking isn't just information that food is coming — it's its own thing, its own enjoyment, and a good cook knows that making people wait a little is part of the meal.

I remember being a child and loving the specific quality of Christmas Eve over Christmas Day. The lights were the same, the decorations were the same, the food was in some ways better on the day itself. But Christmas Eve had something the day didn't. The waiting was so present you could feel its texture, almost see it in the air. Everything was about to happen. Nothing had happened yet. There was a particular silence in that particular waiting, and I remember it more clearly than I remember most Christmas Days.

I think this is what I've been circling around. The waiting is not the absence of experience. It is a specific kind of experience, with its own texture and color and weight. It has moods. It has variety. It reveals things. It is where a significant amount of being alive actually happens, and we have an unfortunate habit of treating it as lost time, as the gaps between the real thing.

But maybe the gaps are the real thing, or part of it. Maybe the structure of a life is not the events but the waiting between them — the quality of attention you bring to the ordinary moments, the texture of the time you spend moving between one significant thing and the next. The bus stop. The held breath. The slow morning when nothing is scheduled. The long quiet of a Sunday afternoon.

All of that is a life too. Maybe most of a life.

And there's something worth paying attention to in the texture of it, if you're willing to wait long enough to feel it.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
